prompt:
Propose attack candidates for the following argument pairs from a debate on "Minimum Wage".

An attack exists when one argument is logically incompatible with the other's conclusion, undermines a premise the other relies on, or shows that the other's conclusion does not follow even if its premise holds. Merely taking the opposing stance, addressing a different sub-topic, or arguing for the other side is NOT enough. There must be a genuine conflict between the two specific claims.

Pair pro82:con14
  A=[pro82] (pro) Increasing the minimum wage would allow teens to work fewer hours for the same amount of pay, giving teens more time to study.
  B=[con14] (con) The company may start outsourcing jobs to employees in countries that are willing to work for much less than $10.10 per hour.

Pair pro82:con14b
  A=[pro82] (pro) Increasing the minimum wage would allow teens to work fewer hours for the same amount of pay, giving teens more time to study.
  B=[con14b] (con) Outsourcing jobs to cheaper foreign workers would result in fewer jobs for Americans.

Pair pro82:con87
  A=[pro82] (pro) Increasing the minimum wage would allow teens to work fewer hours for the same amount of pay, giving teens more time to study.
  B=[con87] (con) Politicians are playing on public sympathy.

Pair pro82:con87b
  A=[pro82] (pro) Increasing the minimum wage would allow teens to work fewer hours for the same amount of pay, giving teens more time to study.
  B=[con87b] (con) Politicians are exploiting the fact that the general public is too braindead to know what is really happening.

Pair pro82:con87c
  A=[pro82] (pro) Increasing the minimum wage would allow teens to work fewer hours for the same amount of pay, giving teens more time to study.
  B=[con87c] (con) A wage hike is nothing more than a bandage on a massive hemorrhage.

Pair pro82:con70
  A=[pro82] (pro) Increasing the minimum wage would allow teens to work fewer hours for the same amount of pay, giving teens more time to study.
  B=[con70] (con) If the minimum wage is increased, fast-food restaurants would pass on almost 100% of their increased labor costs to consumers.

Pair pro82:con70b
  A=[pro82] (pro) Increasing the minimum wage would allow teens to work fewer hours for the same amount of pay, giving teens more time to study.
  B=[con70b] (con) If the minimum wage is increased, other firms may also pass on almost 100% of their increased labor costs to consumers.

Pair pro82:con12
  A=[pro82] (pro) Increasing the minimum wage would allow teens to work fewer hours for the same amount of pay, giving teens more time to study.
  B=[con12] (con) If businesses must pay their minimum wage employees more, businesses cannot afford to hire as many employees.

Pair pro82:con76
  A=[pro82] (pro) Increasing the minimum wage would allow teens to work fewer hours for the same amount of pay, giving teens more time to study.
  B=[con76] (con) The teenage employment index fell sharply after the July 2009 minimum-wage increase, dropping about 8 % in three months, whereas the previous 8 % drop took over a year.

Pair pro82:con76b
  A=[pro82] (pro) Increasing the minimum wage would allow teens to work fewer hours for the same amount of pay, giving teens more time to study.
  B=[con76b] (con) The 2009 minimum-wage increase significantly reduced teenage employment.

direction    = exactly one of: A_ATTACKS_B / B_ATTACKS_A / NONE
confidence   = integer 0-100: probability that ANY attack exists between the two arguments
  (0 = definitely no attack, 100 = definitely attack)
  If direction=NONE, confidence MUST be 0.

Respond ONLY with a JSON array, same order as the pairs above, no markdown:
[
  {"pair": "id_a:id_b", "direction": "A_ATTACKS_B", "confidence": 85, "reason": "one sentence"},
  ...
]
raw_response:
[
  {"pair":"pro82:con14","direction":"B_ATTACKS_A","confidence":80,"reason":"Con14 argues outsourcing will eliminate teen jobs, contradicting pro82’s premise that teens will still have work to cut hours."},
  {"pair":"pro82:con14b","direction":"B_ATTACKS_A","confidence":80,"reason":"Con14b claims outsourcing reduces American jobs, undermining pro82’s assumption that teens can keep jobs to work fewer hours."},
  {"pair":"pro82:con87","direction":"NONE","confidence":0,"reason":"Con87 attacks political motives, not the logical link between minimum wage and teen study time."},
  {"pair":"pro82:con87b","direction":"NONE","confidence":0,"reason":"Con87b focuses on alleged exploitation by politicians, which does not conflict with pro82’s claim."},
  {"pair":"pro82:con87c","direction":"NONE","confidence":0,"reason":"Con87c’s claim that a wage hike is a bandage does not directly refute the specific effect on teen work hours."},
  {"pair":"pro82:con70","direction":"NONE","confidence":0,"reason":"Con70 discusses price pass‑through, not the availability of teen jobs or study time."},
  {"pair":"pro82:con70b","direction":"NONE","confidence":0,"reason":"Con70b also addresses consumer price effects, not the premise about teen employment."},
  {"pair":"pro82:con12","direction":"B_ATTACKS_A","confidence":85,"reason":"Con12 argues higher wages reduce hiring capacity, challenging pro82’s premise that teens will retain jobs to reduce hours."},
  {"pair":"pro82:con76","direction":"B_ATTACKS_A","confidence":90,"reason":"Con76 provides empirical evidence of a sharp drop in teen employment after a wage hike, contradicting pro82’s premise."},
  {"pair":"pro82:con76b","direction":"B_ATTACKS_A","confidence":90,"reason":"Con76b asserts the 2009 increase significantly cut teenage employment, directly undermining pro82’s claim."}
]

parsed.reason: Con14 argues outsourcing will eliminate teen jobs, contradicting pro82’s premise that teens will still have work to cut hours.
Con14b claims outsourcing reduces American jobs, undermining pro82’s assumption that teens can keep jobs to work fewer hours.
Con87 attacks political motives, not the logical link between minimum wage and teen study time.
Con87b focuses on alleged exploitation by politicians, which does not conflict with pro82’s claim.
Con87c’s claim that a wage hike is a bandage does not directly refute the specific effect on teen work hours.
Con70 discusses price pass‑through, not the availability of teen jobs or study time.
Con70b also addresses consumer price effects, not the premise about teen employment.
Con12 argues higher wages reduce hiring capacity, challenging pro82’s premise that teens will retain jobs to reduce hours.
Con76 provides empirical evidence of a sharp drop in teen employment after a wage hike, contradicting pro82’s premise.
Con76b asserts the 2009 increase significantly cut teenage employment, directly undermining pro82’s claim.
